EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across Arizona increasing the winds in the Eastern Deserts of Southeastern Arizona. The strong winds lofted up blowing dust, decreasing visibilities to dangerous levels leading to the closure of Interstate 10 and a car crash along Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across Arizona increasing the winds in the Eastern Deserts of Southeastern Arizona. The strong winds lofted up blowing dust, decreasing visibilities to dangerous levels leading to the closure of Interstate 10 and a car crash along Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category was introduced to northeastern sections of southeast Arizona during beginning of February and by the end of the month included western sections and eastern sections of Southeastern Arizona. The total area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County; and Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. Across Southeast Arizona, the overall precipitation ranking was in the bottom tenth of the driest months on record. Soil moisture readings fell to below the 1st percentile ranking by the end of February in most areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category was introduced to northeastern sections of southeast Arizona during beginning of February. The drought area began with portions of southern Greenlee and southeast Graham counties. For the month of February, Greenlee County ranked 10th driest and Graham ranked the 14th driest for the period of record. Soil moisture readings is between the 5th and 10th percentile for the month of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved through the Desert Southwest, generating strong winds across much of the region. Peak afternoon and early evening wind gusts upwards of 25-40 mph out of the south were observed across much of south-central AZ. The combination of the strong winds and the very dry soil conditions led to areas of blowing dust with some areas across southern Maricopa and Northern Pinal Counties reporting near zero visibility. Near Tonopah AZ, a fiery multi-vehicle collision pileup resulting in multiple fatalities and injuries occurred on the eastbound lanes of I-10. A shallow plume of dense blowing dust was observed on satellite imagery during the time of the accident, and although not officially confirmed, the dust is believed to have played a major role in the cause of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed near zero visibility along State Route 347 near Riggs Road due to dense blowing dust from strong winds. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

This was marked by an intense band of snow that was short lived, but dropped quite a bit of snow.  This created travel problems including a large traffic pile up on Interstate 40 near Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started falling across the Western Mogollon Rim by late morning on Thursday March 13. A snow squall started moving across the zone around 320 PM with strong winds (40 MPH +) and very low visibility. An 18 to 22 car pile-up occured west bound on Interstate 40 at mile post 159 just west of Williams. The wreck started a fire that burned for up to 22 hours. Initally, two people were thought to have died. The remains of three other people were later discovered in the ashes as the result of a missing persons report. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved through the Desert Southwest, generating strong winds across much of the region. Peak afternoon and early evening wind gusts upwards of 25-40 mph out of the south were observed across much of south-central AZ. The combination of the strong winds and the very dry soil conditions led to areas of blowing dust with some areas across southern Maricopa and Northern Pinal Counties reporting near zero visibility. Near Tonopah AZ, a fiery multi-vehicle collision pileup resulting in multiple fatalities and injuries occurred on the eastbound lanes of I-10. A shallow plume of dense blowing dust was observed on satellite imagery during the time of the accident, and although not officially confirmed, the dust is believed to have played a major role in the cause of the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fiery multi-vehicle collision pileup occurred on the eastbound lanes of I-10 near Tonopah, AZ. A shallow plume of dense blowing dust was observed on satellite imagery during the time of the accident, and although not officially confirmed, the dust is believed to have played a major role in the cause of the accident. Based on several news articles, twelve vehicles in total, including multiple semi-trucks, passengers vehicles, a van, and a recreational vehicle were involved in the accident. Four confirmed fatalities and eight injuries resulted from the collision pileup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously deep, slow moving low pressure system moved through the Desert Southwest throughout the day on the 4th. The strong forcing for ascent in combination with above normal moisture values resulted in widespread scattered shower activity across much south-central AZ. MUCAPE values upwards of 500 J/KG resulted in pockets of heavier rainfall rates with some training of the shower activity. An area that observed these training of showers was along State Route 238, where pockets of greater than one inch were observed based on radar estimates. The rainfall activity was enough to result in closures of the highway in some sectors due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 238 near the intersection with South 99th Avenue in Mobile, AZ was closed due to flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large-scale low pressure system moving through the western United States resulted in an expansive area of scattered shower activity across much of south-central AZ during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 2nd. With the cold core aloft moving through the region, steep mid-level lapse rates resulted in MUCAPE values between 100-200 J/KG, enough for some of these showers to contain embedded lightning strikes as well as small hail. One of these showers moved through the Buckeye area and produced a brief landspout tornado over an open field as shown by video footage. No damages or injuries were reported by the landspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video footage showed a landspout tornado over an open field near the intersection of Johnson and Yuma Road in Buckeye. Given that this landspout developed over an open field, no damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front in association with an upper-level low pressure system moved southward through south-central AZ during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 18th, producing an expansive area of showers and isolated thunderstorms. As these showers moved across the region, they produced strong wind gusts in excess of 40 mph across portions of the region, with isolated reports of some minor damage as well as blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medium-sized tree was uprooted in the backyard of a house in Gilbert, with the tree falling on the back of a parked pickup truck. Timing estimated from radar with Mesa Gateway Airport measuring a peak gust of 46 mph at around the same time. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture plume from a tropical system with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.4, which for early June is near record levels, combined with a series of low pressure systems that affected the Desert Southwest to result in periods of showers and thunderstorms between June 1-4. The first round of rainfall activity was observed across south-central AZ during the overnight hours of June 1-2, with observed amounts of 1-2 inches across northern portions of the Phoenix area towards the foothills and higher terrain areas to the north and east. The rainfall was enough to result in flood impacts near the Wildcat Burn Scar located east of Carefree. Another round of rainfall activity was observed during the overnight hours of June 3-4 across portions of La Paz County in association with a secondary low pressure system that resulted in a road closure near Parker due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos taken along Bartlett Dam Rd show evidence of flash flooding over the road in multiple spots, including at the Camp Creek crossing, with many small boulders, sediment, and other debris left over in the road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual early June scenario occurred as a cutoff upper level low off the California coast interacted with residual tropical moisture brought up into Arizona from what was once Tropical Storm Alvin. Moisture combine with the low pressure system to trigger thunderstorms over northern Arizona, with some storms training in the same spot for long periods of time.  This led to areas of flash flooding on the Havasupai Reservation and portions of the Grand Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS Stream Gage indicated a rise of over 6 feet in Kanab Creek due to heavy rain upstream.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tribal officials from the Havasupai Reservation reported that trails were flooded and impassable along Havasu Creek causing hikers to be stranded.  All hikers were later accounted for, no injuries or fatalities.  Some structures in the village of Supai were also impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper level low pressure system traversed across Arizona on June 4th.  As it did, it tapped leftover moisture from what was Tropical Storm Alvin, and triggered some strong thunderstorms.  One of the storms produced a brief landspout tornado in Apache County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via social media a video of a landspout tornado out in a rural field about 5 miles NNE of Springerville.  No damage was reported.  Tornado was rated EFU, but could be an EF0.  No damage survey was done.  Path is approximated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture plume from a tropical system with precipitable water values between 1.0-1.4, which for early June is near record levels, combined with a series of low pressure systems that affected the Desert Southwest to result in periods of showers and thunderstorms between June 1-4. The first round of rainfall activity was observed across south-central AZ during the overnight hours of June 1-2, with observed amounts of 1-2 inches across northern portions of the Phoenix area towards the foothills and higher terrain areas to the north and east. The rainfall was enough to result in flood impacts near the Wildcat Burn Scar located east of Carefree. Another round of rainfall activity was observed during the overnight hours of June 3-4 across portions of La Paz County in association with a secondary low pressure system that resulted in a road closure near Parker due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of 4th Street and California Avenue in Parker was closed due to flooding. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the subtropical high over the Four Corners/Rockies and an area of low pressure near the southern CA coastline resulted in a modest southeasterly flow that advected modest amounts of moisture across much of central and southern AZ. With the moisture advection, PWAT values increased upwards of 1.3-1.5. This moisture quantity in combination with strong daytime heating led to moderate instability as MLCAPE increased upwards of 1000 J/KG. Thunderstorm activity during the early to mid afternoon hours on the 2nd developed across the higher terrain areas of south-central and southeast AZ with outflow boundaries migrating west to northwestward into the lower deserts, where additional thunderstorm activity developed, with the heaviest activity observed across the western half of Maricopa County through the western side of the Phoenix metro area. Another isolated cluster of thunderstorms developed across central La Paz County as well. The increasing upper-level forcing from the approaching low pressure system likely enhanced the thunderstorm activity. During the afternoon hours on the 3rd, additional thunderstorm activity, although much more isolated in nature, developed across portions of south-central AZ. This activity was aided by moderate instability as MLCAPE values were around 1000-1500 J/KG and the presence of the upper-level forcing from the aforementioned low pressure system as it was traversing the region. With DCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG throughout the entire time period, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong downburst winds as well as dense blowing dust. With PWATs values approaching 1.5, the environment was moist enough for heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms that would result in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RAWS station located along the Belmont Mountain measured a peak wind gust of 85 mph.

Thunderstorm activity during the early to mid afternoon hours on the 2nd developed across the higher terrain areas of south-central and southeast AZ with outflow boundaries migrating west to northwestward into the lower deserts, where additional thunderstorm activity developed, with the heaviest activity observed across the western half of Maricopa County through the western side of the Phoenix metro area. Another isolated cluster of thunderstorms developed across central La Paz County as well. The increasing upper-level forcing from the approaching low pressure system likely enhanced the thunderstorm activity. During the afternoon hours on the 3rd, additional thunderstorm activity, although much more isolated in nature, developed across portions of south-central AZ. This activity was aided by moderate instability as MLCAPE values were around 1000-1500 J/KG and the presence of the upper-level forcing from the aforementioned low pressure system as it was traversing the region. With DCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG throughout the entire time period, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong downburst winds as well as dense blowing dust. With PWATs values approaching 1.5, the environment was moist enough for heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms that would result in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Live shot from local broadcast station showed visibility dropping to around a quarter mile due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the subtropical high over the Four Corners/Rockies and an area of low pressure near the southern CA coastline resulted in a modest southeasterly flow that advected modest amounts of moisture across much of central and southern AZ. With the moisture advection, PWAT values increased upwards of 1.3-1.5. This moisture quantity in combination with strong daytime heating led to moderate instability as MLCAPE increased upwards of 1000 J/KG. Thunderstorm activity during the early to mid afternoon hours on the 2nd developed across the higher terrain areas of south-central and southeast AZ with outflow boundaries migrating west to northwestward into the lower deserts, where additional thunderstorm activity developed, with the heaviest activity observed across the western half of Maricopa County through the western side of the Phoenix metro area. Another isolated cluster of thunderstorms developed across central La Paz County as well. The increasing upper-level forcing from the approaching low pressure system likely enhanced the thunderstorm activity. During the afternoon hours on the 3rd, additional thunderstorm activity, although much more isolated in nature, developed across portions of south-central AZ. This activity was aided by moderate instability as MLCAPE values were around 1000-1500 J/KG and the presence of the upper-level forcing from the aforementioned low pressure system as it was traversing the region. With DCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/KG throughout the entire time period, conditions were favorable for the generation of strong downburst winds as well as dense blowing dust. With PWATs values approaching 1.5, the environment was moist enough for heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms that would result in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed reduced visibilities of around a quarter mile along 1-10 near Casa Grande due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accident nor injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received social media video of a distant vortex or landspout tornado.  It formed along intersecting outflow boundaries.  Inconclusive whether the rotating column extended to the base of the cloud.  Exact duration unknown but likely very short lived.  Damage unknown but likely none to minimal as it was out in open desert.

Two individuals deaths were heat caused, one in Tucson and one in Topawa. An Extreme Heat Warning was in effect for all the lower elevations, excluding eastern Cochise County.

The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 2 individuals, one in Sells and one in Topawa. One was heat contributed and one was heat caused.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment across south-central and southwest Arizona on August 25th was primed for strong to severe thunderstorms with very moist and unstable conditions in place. The synoptic pattern featured a subtropical ridge of high pressure centered over northern New Mexico, promoting southeasterly flow aloft over Arizona and allowing for moisture advection into the region, while a Pacific trough to the west promoted upper level divergence over the region. The broad ascent over the region combined with MLCAPE values around 1,000 J/kg and DCAPE values also around 1,000 J/kg provided an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorm development. Daytime heating allowed for continued destabilization with thunderstorms initiating near the southern Arizona/Mexico international early in the afternoon. These thunderstorms then raced toward the north through the afternoon, producing very strong outflow winds that resulted in a large wall of dust. The large dust storm continued to progress toward the north into the Phoenix metro by late afternoon/early evening. The dust storm resulted in widespread visibilities under a quarter mile. The strong outflow boundary responsible for the dust storm led to additional thunderstorm development with some of these thunderstorms becoming strong to severe in the Phoenix metro. No injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility near Mesa Drive and McKellips Road was reported to be less than 1/4 mile due to dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical high was situated near the Four Corners area, producing an easterly steering flow of 15-20 kts across the region. Thunderstorm activity erupted near the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours and was steered west to west-southwestward into the lower deserts of south-central AZ, where new thunderstorm activity developed along outflow boundaries. With MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, the environment was favorable for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst wind gusts as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the dust prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines in Apache Junction. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southwestern portions of the Phoenix area, near the Avondale and Goodyear area and then migrated northeastward into the north Phoenix and Scottsdale areas. With MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values of 1000 J/KG, it was enough to support isolated damaging wind gusts. In addition, with PWATs of around 1.5, the environment was sufficiently conducive to support some heavy rainfall rates as well, in excess of 0.5 per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge along East Fork Cave Creek near 7th Avenue in North Phoenix showed water levels rose above the alarm stage level of 3 feet, with the flow peaking at 4.09 feet. This increase in water levels above alarm stage likely caused the low-water crossing along 7th Avenue immediately downstream of the gauge location to become impassable. No damages or injuries were reported.

These storms caused severe wind gusts and lofted blowing dust. Additionally, there was heavy rainfall causing flash flooding causing a roadway to close for 11 hours and stranded a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded gust of 60 MPH. This wind was able to loft areas of blowing dust that reduced visibility to 3 to 5 statute miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflows from strong thunderstorms lofted areas of blowing dust that caused a formation of a dust storm. This dust storm began in the White Mountains and New Mexico and moved south-southwest into Greenlee County, then moved across Cochise County. This dust storm reduced visibilities to less than 0.25 miles with no injury, fatality, or damage to property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post on social media showed dust storm reducing visibilities to less than 0.25 miles near Portal, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved westward on the evening of August 20th, 2025 that produced a wind gust of 59 mph and damaged weather equipment that caused a power outage for an estimated 1,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power outage was caused by damage to 1,000 customers for an unknown duration of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment across south-central and southwest Arizona on August 25th was primed for strong to severe thunderstorms with very moist and unstable conditions in place. The synoptic pattern featured a subtropical ridge of high pressure centered over northern New Mexico, promoting southeasterly flow aloft over Arizona and allowing for moisture advection into the region, while a Pacific trough to the west promoted upper level divergence over the region. The broad ascent over the region combined with MLCAPE values around 1,000 J/kg and DCAPE values also around 1,000 J/kg provided an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorm development. Daytime heating allowed for continued destabilization with thunderstorms initiating near the southern Arizona/Mexico international early in the afternoon. These thunderstorms then raced toward the north through the afternoon, producing very strong outflow winds that resulted in a large wall of dust. The large dust storm continued to progress toward the north into the Phoenix metro by late afternoon/early evening. The dust storm resulted in widespread visibilities under a quarter mile. The strong outflow boundary responsible for the dust storm led to additional thunderstorm development with some of these thunderstorms becoming strong to severe in the Phoenix metro. No injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility near Bowlin Road and Porter Road was reported to be less than 1/4 mile due to dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical high was situated near the Four Corners area, producing an easterly steering flow of 15-20 kts across the region. Thunderstorm activity erupted near the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours and was steered west to west-southwestward into the lower deserts of south-central AZ, where new thunderstorm activity developed along outflow boundaries. With MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, the environment was favorable for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst wind gusts as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the dust prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Camera from the Arizona Department of Transportation showed visibility of less than 1/4 mile in the area of State Route 347 and Casa Blanca Road due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level trough situated across the west coast and the subtropical high pressure situated over the Central Plains resulted in a south to southwest flow across the region. The combination of 500 mb temperatures near -8C and PWATs ranging between 1.3-1.5 inches resulted in MLCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/KG. A Mesoscale Convective Complex moving northward from Mexico triggered showers and thunderstorms across portions of western Pinal and southwestern Maricopa Counties with more isolated activity across northern La Paz County during the mid and late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-8 near State Route 85 in Gila Bend closed due to all lanes being blocked due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the subtropical high pressure over Colorado and an upper trough over west coast led to south-southwest flow over much of the region. Thunderstorm activity during the afternoon hours developed across southwest Maricopa County as well as across the Mogollon Rim area. By the mid to late evening hours, colliding outflows from these storm complexes led to the development of additional thunderstorm activity across the Phoenix area, especially across the central and northern portions of the metro. With MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values near 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for these storms to produce strong to locally severe wind gusts. In addition, the thunderstorm activity due to very light steering flow was also slowing moving, leading to a flash flood threat with some areas in the heaviest activity measuring in excess of 1-2 inches of rain in a short time span. Additional thunderstorm activity continued into the overnight period with coverage becoming more widespread across much of south-central AZ as leading shortwave trough originating from the main upper-trough over the west coast helped to sustain the activity. Some areas across northwestern Pinal County, including the City of Maricopa, measured rainfall amounts in excess of 2 inches in a 3-5 hour time span, resulting in some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge located along East Fork Cave Creek near 7th Avenue showed water levels rising above the alarm stage of 3 feet likely flooding 7th avenue just downstream. Water levels peaked at 3.90 feet at around 12 AM MST before gradually rising below alarm stage at around 1:23 AM MST. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southwestern portions of the Phoenix area, near the Avondale and Goodyear area and then migrated northeastward into the north Phoenix and Scottsdale areas. With MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values of 1000 J/KG, it was enough to support isolated damaging wind gusts. In addition, with PWATs of around 1.5, the environment was sufficiently conducive to support some heavy rainfall rates as well, in excess of 0.5 per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees uprooted along the roads near Fashion Square Mall in Scottsdale, including a couple of trees along Camelback Road between 64th and 68th street. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

The overall precipitation for Arizona ranking was 16th driest for the month of August. Soil moisture readings decreased to between the 1st percentile to 10th percentile ranking for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, Graham county ranked the 4th driest for the period of record and Greenlee county ranked the 17th driest for the period of record. Soil moisture readings increased to between the 5th and 10th percentile for the month of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms occurred on the afternoon and evening of August 15th, 2025 that produced nickel sized (0.88 inch) hail in San Simon, AZ. Additionally, heavy rainfall from thunderstorms caused flash flooding and closed Frontier Road in Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture from sheriff showing cars in flooded on Frontier road with water nearly up the height of the tire. This flooding closed the road for an unknown duration of time. A nearby rain gauge recorded 0.08 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved northwest through Southeastern Arizona the afternoon and evening of August 25th. These storms caused the formation of a dust storm that decreased visibilities to less than 0.25 miles, caused damage to utility equipment causing power outages for an estimated total of 1283 customers, and caused damage to a roof. Additionally, the heavy rainfall from the thunderstorms caused cars to be stranded along Frontage Road in Tucson due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video of cars stuck in flooded waters on frontage road along Interstate-10  north of Red Rock. A nearby rain gauge reported 1.83 inches of rainfall fell in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm on the afternoon of August 16th, 2025 caused flash flooding in the Tucson Metro. Water flooded the streets from curb to curb, roughly 6 inches of moving water, occurred in multiple locations. A nearby rain gauge reported 1.26 inches of rainfall fell in less than 1 hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed water from curb to curb and rushing with an estimated 6 inches of moving water along Mabel St from 2nd to 3rd Ave. A nearby rain gauge reported 1.26 inches of rainfall fell in less than 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment across south-central and southwest Arizona on August 25th was primed for strong to severe thunderstorms with very moist and unstable conditions in place. The synoptic pattern featured a subtropical ridge of high pressure centered over northern New Mexico, promoting southeasterly flow aloft over Arizona and allowing for moisture advection into the region, while a Pacific trough to the west promoted upper level divergence over the region. The broad ascent over the region combined with MLCAPE values around 1,000 J/kg and DCAPE values also around 1,000 J/kg provided an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorm development. Daytime heating allowed for continued destabilization with thunderstorms initiating near the southern Arizona/Mexico international early in the afternoon. These thunderstorms then raced toward the north through the afternoon, producing very strong outflow winds that resulted in a large wall of dust. The large dust storm continued to progress toward the north into the Phoenix metro by late afternoon/early evening. The dust storm resulted in widespread visibilities under a quarter mile. The strong outflow boundary responsible for the dust storm led to additional thunderstorm development with some of these thunderstorms becoming strong to severe in the Phoenix metro. No injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down along Avenue 29 E at County 9th Street, County 13th Street, and County 14 Street. Timing estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the subtropical high pressure over Colorado and an upper trough over west coast led to south-southwest flow over much of the region. Thunderstorm activity during the afternoon hours developed across southwest Maricopa County as well as across the Mogollon Rim area. By the mid to late evening hours, colliding outflows from these storm complexes led to the development of additional thunderstorm activity across the Phoenix area, especially across the central and northern portions of the metro. With MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values near 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for these storms to produce strong to locally severe wind gusts. In addition, the thunderstorm activity due to very light steering flow was also slowing moving, leading to a flash flood threat with some areas in the heaviest activity measuring in excess of 1-2 inches of rain in a short time span. Additional thunderstorm activity continued into the overnight period with coverage becoming more widespread across much of south-central AZ as leading shortwave trough originating from the main upper-trough over the west coast helped to sustain the activity. Some areas across northwestern Pinal County, including the City of Maricopa, measured rainfall amounts in excess of 2 inches in a 3-5 hour time span, resulting in some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video in a parking lot of local shopping area near the area of I-17 and Happy Valley Road in North Phoenix showed large tree branches snapped from a thunderstorm microburst. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rain caused some flash flooding in Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS stream gauge on the Agua Fria River near Humboldt (AFHA3) recorded a rise of 5.91 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mesoscale convective system formed over northern Mexico and moved north into Arizona.  This feature produced severe weather in southern Arizona and a large haboob dust storm that tracked all the way into southern Yavapai County.  The following thunderstorms progressed through northern Arizona and produced heavy rain and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ADOT webcam showed visibility reduced to an estimated 3/4 of a mile on I-17 near Sunset Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture led to multiple thunderstorms throughout the day.  Some of the storms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS river gauge on the Verde River below Camp Verde (VCVA3) recorded a 4 foot rise (1,650 cfs) in an hour due to heavy rainfall upstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture led to multiple thunderstorms throughout the day.  Some of the storms produced heavy rain that led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Yavapai County ALERT gauge on the Agua Fria River at Kelton Ranch reported a 2.7 foot  rise in 15 minutes from heavy rainfall in Big Bug Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the subtropical high pressure over Colorado and an upper trough over west coast led to south-southwest flow over much of the region. Thunderstorm activity during the afternoon hours developed across southwest Maricopa County as well as across the Mogollon Rim area. By the mid to late evening hours, colliding outflows from these storm complexes led to the development of additional thunderstorm activity across the Phoenix area, especially across the central and northern portions of the metro. With MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/KG and DCAPE values near 1500 J/KG, conditions were favorable for these storms to produce strong to locally severe wind gusts. In addition, the thunderstorm activity due to very light steering flow was also slowing moving, leading to a flash flood threat with some areas in the heaviest activity measuring in excess of 1-2 inches of rain in a short time span. Additional thunderstorm activity continued into the overnight period with coverage becoming more widespread across much of south-central AZ as leading shortwave trough originating from the main upper-trough over the west coast helped to sustain the activity. Some areas across northwestern Pinal County, including the City of Maricopa, measured rainfall amounts in excess of 2 inches in a 3-5 hour time span, resulting in some flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows fast flowing water on the roadway, likely at least 6 inches, in the area of Bell Road and 56th Street in Scottsdale. Timing estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were near 5 to 9 degrees above normal with Moderate to Major HeatRisk. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that the cause of death for 7 individuals. Five were heat contributed, two in Tucson, one in Sells, one in Safford, and one in Hereford. Two individuals deaths were heat caused, one in Tucson and one in Topawa. An Extreme Heat Warning was in effect for all the lower elevations, excluding eastern Cochise County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 8 degrees above normal, causing Major HeatRisk. The Tucson International Airport reached a high temperature of 107 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 2 individuals in Tucson, one was heat contributed and one was heat caused.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical high was situated near the Four Corners area, producing an easterly steering flow of 15-20 kts across the region. Thunderstorm activity erupted near the Mogollon Rim during the early afternoon hours and was steered west to west-southwestward into the lower deserts of south-central AZ, where new thunderstorm activity developed along outflow boundaries. With MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG and DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG, the environment was favorable for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe downburst wind gusts as well as areas of dense blowing dust across the dust prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images showed power poles toppled near Gold Canyon. Location and timing based on outage map report. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of tropical moisture from tropical storms Pricilla and Raymond allowed for deep moisture as well as showers and thunderstorms to form from October 11th through October 14th. Severe thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on October 11th causing multiple reports of 1 inch hail and uprooted trees on the southeast side of the metro. Additionally, as this storm moved northward caused damage to electrical equipment causing a power outage to 856 customers for 18 hours. Multiple rounds of light to locally heavy rainfall on September 12th caused washes to run with water in the Tucson metro causing 9 swift water rescues with no injuries. Additionally, there were multiple reports of a funnel cloud near Drexel Heights. On October 13th there was an isolated thunderstorm and showers that caused flow in washes through October 14th. This led to a swift water rescue in Eloy with no injuries. The 4 day rainfall totals ranged from 0.5 inches to locally up to 4.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1.0 inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) drought category ended in the far eastern section of Southeastern Arizona. The total area from portions of Graham, Greenlee, and Cochise counties. The overall precipitation for Arizona ranking was 19th driest for the period of record for the month of October. Soil moisture readings increased to between the 30th and 70th percentile ranking for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of October, Graham county ranked the 12th driest for the period of record and Greenlee county ranked the 15th driest for the period of record. Soil moisture readings increased to range between the 30th and 70th percentile for the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper-level trough combined with tropical moisture remnants from Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond, which developed off the eastern Pacific, led to scattered to widespread thunderstorm activity across much of south-central AZ. Beginning late on the 8th through the 9th, very anomalous moisture from Tropical Cyclone Priscilla surged northward with PWATs rising to over 1.5+, which was an all-time record for early October. Light to moderate rainfall developed, with the most widespread activity occurring from the afternoon of the 10th into the early afternoon of the 11th. Even though rainfall rates were light with no flooding occurring with this initial activity, it saturated the soils for the heavy rainfall that would develop hours later. ||Heading into the pre-dawn hours on the 12th, another moisture surge, this time from the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Raymond, combined with strong upper-level ascent from the aforementioned low pressure system off the west coast to trigger scattered thunderstorm activity across much of south-central AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours. With MLCAPE values having increased to around 1000 J/KG, the environment was conducive to high rainfall rates in excess of 1-2+ an hour. These high rainfall rates falling on top of saturated soils resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across much of south-central AZ. ||Another round of scattered thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours on the 13th. With MLCAPE values peaking at 1000 J/KG and deep-layered shear values approaching 50 kts, the environment became conducive for the development of severe thunderstorms, including supercells. One such supercell affected the Chandler, Tempe, and Scottsdale areas with the city of Tempe being severely hit with extensive wind damage from large, mature trees uprooted or snapped, some of which damaged buildings and vehicles, to downed powerlines, and roofs blown off buildings. A damage survey conducted by some NWS Phoenix Meteorologists supported wind gusts with this supercell peaking around 80-90 mph. Other storms that developed through the afternoon and early evening resulted in large hail and more instances of flash flooding. By the end of the multi-day event, rainfall totals ranged between 1.00-1.50��� across the Phoenix West Valley to as high as 3.50-4.50��� across the Phoenix East Valley, with locations across the foothills and higher terrain areas observing total amounts of 2.50-6.00���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 6 between U.S. 70 and San Carlos closed due to flooding from Gilson Wash with a community near Milepost 6-7 evacuated. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level low moving through the Desert Southwest combined with above normal moisture (PWATs upwards of 0.8) to generate scattered showers and a few embedded thunderstorms across portions of south-central AZ during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Even though the shower activity was not impactful at all, an isolated thunderstorm moving through the Avondale area just after 0400MST generated cloud to ground lightning that sparked a small house fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike resulted in a small house fire near the area of El Mirage and Lower Buckeye Roads in Avondale. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

A couple of water rescues were performed near the vicinity of the wash, one near Narramore Road and another near Tuthill Road. One of the flood incidents along the wash turned deadly as a man was found deceased after the pickup truck he was driving was washed away from the floodwaters. The pickup truck was found less than a mile downstream with the man not found, requiring a search and rescue operation to be activated. The body the man was eventually found by the search and rescue team approximately 34 hours after the reported incident. Timing was estimated based on radar and the nearby stream gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of tropical moisture from tropical storms Pricilla and Raymond allowed for deep moisture as well as showers and thunderstorms to form from October 11th through October 14th. Severe thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on October 11th causing multiple reports of 1 inch hail and uprooted trees on the southeast side of the metro. Additionally, as this storm moved northward caused damage to electrical equipment causing a power outage to 856 customers for 18 hours. Multiple rounds of light to locally heavy rainfall on September 12th caused washes to run with water in the Tucson metro causing 9 swift water rescues with no injuries. Additionally, there were multiple reports of a funnel cloud near Drexel Heights. On October 13th there was an isolated thunderstorm and showers that caused flow in washes through October 14th. This led to a swift water rescue in Eloy with no injuries. The 4 day rainfall totals ranged from 0.5 inches to locally up to 4.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old Ajo Hy west of San Joaquin Rd closed due to flooding for an unknown duration of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS stream gage on the Moenkopi Wash (MOEA3) measured an 8 foot rise in 3 hours, from 18.69 to 26.74 ft.  This would put the Moenkopi Wash into moderate flood stage. The Moenkopi Wash would further rise into major flood stage, cresting at a stage of 28.81 ft and 4867 cfs between 6:45 pm and 7:30 pm before beginning to recede.

Multiple rounds of light to locally heavy rainfall on September 12th caused washes to run with water in the Tucson metro causing 9 swift water rescues with no injuries. Additionally, there were multiple reports of a funnel cloud near Drexel Heights. On October 13th there was an isolated thunderstorm and showers that caused flow in washes through October 14th. This led to a swift water rescue in Eloy with no injuries. The 4 day rainfall totals ranged from 0.5 inches to locally up to 4.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcam shows water over bankfull at the McClellan Wash at the Central Arizona Project Wash.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinal County Sherriff reported water rescue  in flood waters most of the way up the  stranded car. Rescue was on Battaglia Drive between Highway 87 and La Palma road.  Battaglia Drive was reported to have been  already closed due to flood waters. Rainfall occurred afternoon of October 13 and a Flash Flood Warning had been previously issued with those thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper-level trough combined with tropical moisture remnants from Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond, which developed off the eastern Pacific, led to scattered to widespread thunderstorm activity across much of south-central AZ. Beginning late on the 8th through the 9th, very anomalous moisture from Tropical Cyclone Priscilla surged northward with PWATs rising to over 1.5+, which was an all-time record for early October. Light to moderate rainfall developed, with the most widespread activity occurring from the afternoon of the 10th into the early afternoon of the 11th. Even though rainfall rates were light with no flooding occurring with this initial activity, it saturated the soils for the heavy rainfall that would develop hours later. ||Heading into the pre-dawn hours on the 12th, another moisture surge, this time from the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Raymond, combined with strong upper-level ascent from the aforementioned low pressure system off the west coast to trigger scattered thunderstorm activity across much of south-central AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours. With MLCAPE values having increased to around 1000 J/KG, the environment was conducive to high rainfall rates in excess of 1-2+ an hour. These high rainfall rates falling on top of saturated soils resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across much of south-central AZ. ||Another round of scattered thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours on the 13th. With MLCAPE values peaking at 1000 J/KG and deep-layered shear values approaching 50 kts, the environment became conducive for the development of severe thunderstorms, including supercells. One such supercell affected the Chandler, Tempe, and Scottsdale areas with the city of Tempe being severely hit with extensive wind damage from large, mature trees uprooted or snapped, some of which damaged buildings and vehicles, to downed powerlines, and roofs blown off buildings. A damage survey conducted by some NWS Phoenix Meteorologists supported wind gusts with this supercell peaking around 80-90 mph. Other storms that developed through the afternoon and early evening resulted in large hail and more instances of flash flooding. By the end of the multi-day event, rainfall totals ranged between 1.00-1.50��� across the Phoenix West Valley to as high as 3.50-4.50��� across the Phoenix East Valley, with locations across the foothills and higher terrain areas observing total amounts of 2.50-6.00���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple social media images posted showed flooding around the Ak-Chin Village area. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Stream Gauge along Skunk Creek Wash near Desert Hills Drive showed water levels rising above the alarm stage level of 2.22 feet, peaking at 2.46 feet (504 cfs), and making all low-water crossings downstream impassable. A water rescue was performed along North 19th Avenue, which is one of the low water-crossings.  Timing was estimated based on the period water levels exceeded and then receded below alarm stage level and when the water rescue incident occurred. No injuries reported.

Beginning late on the 8th through the 9th, very anomalous moisture from Tropical Cyclone Priscilla surged northward with PWATs rising to over 1.5+, which was an all-time record for early October. Light to moderate rainfall developed, with the most widespread activity occurring from the afternoon of the 10th into the early afternoon of the 11th. Even though rainfall rates were light with no flooding occurring with this initial activity, it saturated the soils for the heavy rainfall that would develop hours later. ||Heading into the pre-dawn hours on the 12th, another moisture surge, this time from the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Raymond, combined with strong upper-level ascent from the aforementioned low pressure system off the west coast to trigger scattered thunderstorm activity across much of south-central AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours. With MLCAPE values having increased to around 1000 J/KG, the environment was conducive to high rainfall rates in excess of 1-2+ an hour. These high rainfall rates falling on top of saturated soils resulted in numerous instances of flash flooding across much of south-central AZ. ||Another round of scattered thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours on the 13th. With MLCAPE values peaking at 1000 J/KG and deep-layered shear values approaching 50 kts, the environment became conducive for the development of severe thunderstorms, including supercells. One such supercell affected the Chandler, Tempe, and Scottsdale areas with the city of Tempe being severely hit with extensive wind damage from large, mature trees uprooted or snapped, some of which damaged buildings and vehicles, to downed powerlines, and roofs blown off buildings. A damage survey conducted by some NWS Phoenix Meteorologists supported wind gusts with this supercell peaking around 80-90 mph. Other storms that developed through the afternoon and early evening resulted in large hail and more instances of flash flooding. By the end of the multi-day event, rainfall totals ranged between 1.00-1.50��� across the Phoenix West Valley to as high as 3.50-4.50��� across the Phoenix East Valley, with locations across the foothills and higher terrain areas observing total amounts of 2.50-6.00���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: N. Scottsdale Rd was closed in both directions between E Jomax Rd and E Dynamite Blvd due to excessive runoff. No damages or injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme and widespread wind damage from a supercell thunderstorm was observed in Tempe. The most severe damage was concentrated in a 2-2.5 mile north-south corridor just east of I-10, between Downtown Tempe and Warner Road. A damage assessment, based on 1086 total surveys conducted in the days following the event, determined that over 550 homes and 70 businesses were damaged. Some of the home property damaged included two apartment complexes, located near the area of Mill Avenue and U.S. 60, that had 55 units that were deemed uninhabitable, with 11 additional units having severe damage. In total 89 units were impacted, equating to a total of 267 tenants affected. One mobile home park, located near the area of Baseline and Kyrene Roads, suffered severe damage with 6 mobile homes deemed uninhabitable with 336 additional sustaining severe damage. An industrial park located just across the street from the mobile home park had 63 businesses destroyed/sustain severe damage. Approximately 520 trees were lost in parks alone with many more downed trees, some very large-sized, causing damage to buildings and vehicles and downing powerlines. There were a total of 1500 tons of debris generated from the damage. A damage survey conducted by NWS Phoenix Meteorologists in the most affected areas supported peak wind gusts upwards of 80-90 mph. Given the extensive wind damage, a local state of emergency was declared for Tempe. Total damage cost was estimated at least $1.5 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Stream Gauge along Indian Bend Wash near Shea Boulevard in Scottsdale measured water levels rising above the alarm stage level of 2.0 feet, peaking at 2.65 feet (1485 cfs), and likely flooding the greenbelt and low water-crossing areas downstream. Timing was estimated based on the period water levels exceeded and then receded below alarm stage and time it took to reach the downstream low-water crossings. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of tropical moisture from tropical storms Pricilla and Raymond allowed for deep moisture as well as showers and thunderstorms to form from October 11th through October 14th. Severe thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on October 11th causing multiple reports of 1 inch hail and uprooted trees on the southeast side of the metro. Additionally, as this storm moved northward caused damage to electrical equipment causing a power outage to 856 customers for 18 hours. Multiple rounds of light to locally heavy rainfall on September 12th caused washes to run with water in the Tucson metro causing 9 swift water rescues with no injuries. Additionally, there were multiple reports of a funnel cloud near Drexel Heights. On October 13th there was an isolated thunderstorm and showers that caused flow in washes through October 14th. This led to a swift water rescue in Eloy with no injuries. The 4 day rainfall totals ranged from 0.5 inches to locally up to 4.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reports 1.0 inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds of 20 to 25 mph on Lake Havasu led to waves of one to three feet, which contributed to the drowning of one person.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman fell off a houseboat while the captain was attempting to re-anchor due to windy conditions. She drowned despite rescue efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 3 to 6 degrees above normal, causing Low to Moderate HeatRisk. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 4 individuals. 2 deaths were due to heat contribution in Tucson, while 3 were heat caused in Sells and Benson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 5 degrees above normal, causing Moderate HeatRisk. A Benson CWOP site reached a high temperature of 98 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for an individual in Benson and was heat caused.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 5 degrees above normal, causing Moderate HeatRisk. The Sells RAWS reached a high temperature of 105 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for an individual in Sells and was heat caused.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the evening of November 17th and the evening of the 20th, a slow-moving upper-level low pressure system progressed through the Desert Southwest. The combination of the strong dynamical forcing from the low and well-above normal moisture values (PWATs of 0.80+) led to multiple rounds of scattered to widespread moderate to heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. Most of the rain and impacts fell/occurred on the 18th and 19th, with much of central Arizona receiving rainfall totals of 1.00-2.50 and some areas receiving 3+ inches. This rainfall led to instances of flash flooding and flows in normally dry creeks and washes. Several streamflow gauges across the Flood Control District of Maricopa County's (FCDMC) network exceeded designated alarm stages and a couple gauges in far north Phoenix reached bank full. Several swift water rescues as well as road closures were reported across in or near the washes that exceeded alarm stage/bank full. ||With just enough instability (CAPE values near 500 J/KG) and high wind shear (>50 kts), several severe thunderstorms developed, including a few supercells, in Maricopa County on both the 18th and 19th. Hail was the primary severe weather impact, given the lower freezing levels. There was one report of severe hail (1+ inch in diameter) on the 18th, in Sun City Festival, and multiple reports of quarter size to golf ball size hail on the 19th, in Ahwatukee and Tempe. There was also a lot of accumulating hail, especially on the 19th when a portion of I-10 in Tolleson was covered in several inches of hail. The accumulating hail may have been a contributing factor to a partial roof collapse at Desert Sky Mall.||In addition to the flooding and hail, there were also a few reports of funnel clouds and an EF-0 tornado briefly touched down in Forepaugh, AZ, along US-60 between Aguila and Wickenburg during the afternoon hours on the 18th. The tornado caused a small amount of damage, including flipping an RV trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo shows a few hail stones, with the largest measuring up to 1.00 inch in diameter in the area of Loop 101 and U.S. 60 in Tempe. There was also a lot of 0.5 inch diameter hail reported. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved northeast across the parts of the NW Tucson Metro including Oro Valley. This storm had varying hail sizes from pea (0.25 inch diameter) to nickel (0.88 inch diameter) lasting for roughly 5 minutes and caused no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail lasted for approximately 5 minutes, covered the ground, and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system moved down the California coast, tapping ample moisture and spreading precipitation across the state of Arizona.  This was a relatively warm storm with high snow levels, at or above 6500-7000 feet.  Dynamic support and wind shear with this storm led to some severe thunderstorms.  Heavy rain falling over ground saturated from the previous weekend's storm led to flash flooding and elevated creek flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS stream gauge on the Agua Fria River (AFHA3) near Dewey-Humboldt measured a 3 foot rise. Peak flow was measured at 458 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued in the western, southern, and eastern sections of Southeastern Arizona. The total area from much of Pima County into Graham and Greenlee counties and portions of Santa Cruz and Cochise counties. The overall precipitation for Arizona ranking was 14th driest for the month of July. Soil moisture readings increased to between the 20th percentile to 70th percentile ranking for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) continued in Eastern Cochise County in the month of July. For the month of July, Cochise county ranked the 39th driest for the period of record. Soil moisture readings increased to between the 20th and 30th percentile for the month of July. The Extreme Drought (D3) decreased to Severe Drought after July 24th.

The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 5 individuals, 4 in Tucson and 1 in Vail. 2 deaths were heat caused, while 3 were heat contributed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the evening of November 17th and the evening of the 20th, a slow-moving upper-level low pressure system progressed through the Desert Southwest. The combination of the strong dynamical forcing from the low and well-above normal moisture values (PWATs of 0.80+) led to multiple rounds of scattered to widespread moderate to heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. Most of the rain and impacts fell/occurred on the 18th and 19th, with much of central Arizona receiving rainfall totals of 1.00-2.50 and some areas receiving 3+ inches. This rainfall led to instances of flash flooding and flows in normally dry creeks and washes. Several streamflow gauges across the Flood Control District of Maricopa County's (FCDMC) network exceeded designated alarm stages and a couple gauges in far north Phoenix reached bank full. Several swift water rescues as well as road closures were reported across in or near the washes that exceeded alarm stage/bank full. ||With just enough instability (CAPE values near 500 J/KG) and high wind shear (>50 kts), several severe thunderstorms developed, including a few supercells, in Maricopa County on both the 18th and 19th. Hail was the primary severe weather impact, given the lower freezing levels. There was one report of severe hail (1+ inch in diameter) on the 18th, in Sun City Festival, and multiple reports of quarter size to golf ball size hail on the 19th, in Ahwatukee and Tempe. There was also a lot of accumulating hail, especially on the 19th when a portion of I-10 in Tolleson was covered in several inches of hail. The accumulating hail may have been a contributing factor to a partial roof collapse at Desert Sky Mall.||In addition to the flooding and hail, there were also a few reports of funnel clouds and an EF-0 tornado briefly touched down in Forepaugh, AZ, along US-60 between Aguila and Wickenburg during the afternoon hours on the 18th. The tornado caused a small amount of damage, including flipping an RV trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple photos and videos of a brief cold air funnel cloud as seen from Surprise and Wittman areas. Timing and location based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved southwestward into the Tucson Metro area causing power outages and fire starts due to lightning. Additionally, the storms produced heavy rainfall causing running water over roadways in the Tucson Metro. Heavy rainfall also caused a roadway to close for roughly 24 hours in Benson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit power company equipment and caused power outages at the Pima  County Election Headquarters and several other areas in Midtown Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms in the early morning hours produced between 0.25 to 0.80 inches of rainfall in the lower valley floor and between 1.0 to 2.80 inches in the Catalina and Rincon Foothills. This rainfall caused multiple roads to close in the Tucson Metro and in Benson, AZ. Additionally, utility equipment was damaged due to lightning and caused a power outage for 183 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility equipment was damaged due to lightning and caused a power outage for 183 customers for an unknown duration of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A crash was reported due to low visibility due to dust. A nearby station had visibility drop to 0.14 miles with with wind gusts due to thunderstorm outflow at 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the evening of November 17th and the evening of the 20th, a slow-moving upper-level low pressure system progressed through the Desert Southwest. The combination of the strong dynamical forcing from the low and well-above normal moisture values (PWATs of 0.80+) led to multiple rounds of scattered to widespread moderate to heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms. Most of the rain and impacts fell/occurred on the 18th and 19th, with much of central Arizona receiving rainfall totals of 1.00-2.50 and some areas receiving 3+ inches. This rainfall led to instances of flash flooding and flows in normally dry creeks and washes. Several streamflow gauges across the Flood Control District of Maricopa County's (FCDMC) network exceeded designated alarm stages and a couple gauges in far north Phoenix reached bank full. Several swift water rescues as well as road closures were reported across in or near the washes that exceeded alarm stage/bank full. ||With just enough instability (CAPE values near 500 J/KG) and high wind shear (>50 kts), several severe thunderstorms developed, including a few supercells, in Maricopa County on both the 18th and 19th. Hail was the primary severe weather impact, given the lower freezing levels. There was one report of severe hail (1+ inch in diameter) on the 18th, in Sun City Festival, and multiple reports of quarter size to golf ball size hail on the 19th, in Ahwatukee and Tempe. There was also a lot of accumulating hail, especially on the 19th when a portion of I-10 in Tolleson was covered in several inches of hail. The accumulating hail may have been a contributing factor to a partial roof collapse at Desert Sky Mall.||In addition to the flooding and hail, there were also a few reports of funnel clouds and an EF-0 tornado briefly touched down in Forepaugh, AZ, along US-60 between Aguila and Wickenburg during the afternoon hours on the 18th. The tornado caused a small amount of damage, including flipping an RV trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines leading to power outage reported in the area of North 51st Avenue to North 47th Avenue and West Orangewood Avenue to West Maryland Avenue in Glendale. Based on radar evidence and surface observations, wind gusts were estimated upwards of 40 mph. Saturated loose grounds from previous rainfall likely made power lines more susceptible to go down with sub-severe winds. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines along 21st Avenue between Meadow Drive and Coolbrook Avenue in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph from thunderstorm outflow winds well ahead of dissipating storms. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines in the area of Osborn and Thomas Road between 43rd and 39th Avenue in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph from thunderstorm outflow winds well ahead of dissipating storms. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the northern half of AZ as well as near the International Border during the afternoon hours with more isolated activity developing across the foothills and higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. All of this activity was due to strong upper-level ascent associated with a low pressure system along the west coast and another upper-level disturbance over northern Mexico. Outflow boundaries from the aforementioned activity sparked new thunderstorm activity during the evening hours across much of the south-central AZ lower deserts, including the Phoenix area. The combination of MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/kg and DCAPE values of around 1500 J/kg supported strong to locally severe downburst wind gusts. PWATs were also near 1.5, supporting an environment conducive to high rainfall rates with these thunderstorms and thus some flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to 1/4 mile at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwater from heavy rain northeast of Globe traveled downstream within Ramboz Wash to Gilson Wash and then all the way to San Carlos. Video showed a single individual hanging in a tree above their totaled SUV in the floodwaters. A separate post showed flooding across Route 6 near mile post 7, stemming from the floodwaters in Gilson Wash, leading to the closure of the road. Not far from this location, a streamflow gauge measured an 8.3 ft peak stage height at 2240MST after initially rising beginning around 2125MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the northern half of AZ as well as near the International Border during the afternoon hours with more isolated activity developing across the foothills and higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. All of this activity was due to strong upper-level ascent associated with a low pressure system along the west coast and another upper-level disturbance over northern Mexico. Outflow boundaries from the aforementioned activity sparked new thunderstorm activity during the evening hours across much of the south-central AZ lower deserts, including the Phoenix area. The combination of MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/kg and DCAPE values of around 1500 J/kg supported strong to locally severe downburst wind gusts. PWATs were also near 1.5, supporting an environment conducive to high rainfall rates with these thunderstorms and thus some flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large dead tree fell, damaging a cinderblock wall, a couple of motorcycles, and bringing down a power line in the area of University Drive and McClintock Drive in Tempe. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level trough over the west coast and the subtropical high situated over Texas resulted in a southwesterly steering flow. With PWATs ranging between 1.0-1.2 and MLCAPE values approaching 1000 J/kg, afternoon thunderstorms developed across the eastern third of AZ with some of the activity affecting southern Gila County, including the San Carlos area. With DCAPE in excess of 1000 J/kg, conditions were conducive for these thunderstorms to produce strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station QSCA3 San Carlos No. 1 in San Carlos measured a 62 mph peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level low off the CA coast and the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Mario resulted in a significant moisture increase with PWATs increasing upwards of 1.8-2.0 across most of southwest AZ. The aforementioned features also provided strong upper-level forcing to result in widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  With MLCAPE values maxing out at 1500 J/kg along with the abundant moisture, the thunderstorms were efficient rain producers, resulting in instances of flash flooding. DCAPE values ranging between 1000-1200 J/kg also resulted in instances of severe wind gusts. Further east, across Gila County, additional thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours with cell training resulting in instances of flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 peak wind gust measured at the TT823 Mohawk Dunes RAWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willow Beach was closed to inbound traffic due to significant flash flood damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms, with the greatest activity on the 5th and 6th. Many storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pierce Ferry Road was flooded and closed at Grapevine Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms, with the greatest activity on the 5th and 6th. Many storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rocks and debris washed over Pierce Ferry Road at Palo Alto Drive. One vehicle was stuck in the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rainfall to occur on the Cody Wildfire Burn Scar on September 25th, with the San Manuel Airport Rd rain gauge reported 0.67 inches. A debris flow caused mud and debris to flow to an estimated 4 homes along Mt Lemmon Highway and Cody Loop Road, with one home being impacted by the mud and debris. Time of debris flow was estimated by radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinal County Emergency Manager reported debris flow that caused mud and debris to flow to an estimated 4 homes along Mt Lemmon Highway and Cody Loop Road, with one home being impacted by the mud and debris. Time of debris flow was estimated by radar.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in Southeastern Arizona ranged between 4 degrees below normal to 4 degrees above normal with Low HeatRisk. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a contributing factor in the cause of death for 2 individuals, one in Tucson and one in Nogales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 4 degrees below normal, causing Low HeatRisk. A Nogales CWOP site reached a high temperature of 87 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was the contributing factor in the cause of death for an individual in Nogales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the northern half of AZ as well as near the International Border during the afternoon hours with more isolated activity developing across the foothills and higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. All of this activity was due to strong upper-level ascent associated with a low pressure system along the west coast and another upper-level disturbance over northern Mexico. Outflow boundaries from the aforementioned activity sparked new thunderstorm activity during the evening hours across much of the south-central AZ lower deserts, including the Phoenix area. The combination of MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/kg and DCAPE values of around 1500 J/kg supported strong to locally severe downburst wind gusts. PWATs were also near 1.5, supporting an environment conducive to high rainfall rates with these thunderstorms and thus some flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than 1/4 mile along State Route 347 near Riggs Road due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.
