EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District stream gauge along Indian Bend Wash at Shea Boulevard recorded a water level above the alarm stage level of 2 feet, with the stage peaking at 3.02 feet and likely flooding the downstream greenbelt area of the wash and low water crossings. Timing estimated based on the period when water levels exceeded and then receded below the alarm stage level. No damages or injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District streamflow gauge along Jackrabbit Wash recorded a water level above the alarm stage level of 2.9 feet, with a peak stage of 3.78 feet. Timing estimated based on the period when water levels exceeded and then receded below the alarm stage level as well as travel time for the water to reach Wickenburg Road. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level low off the CA coast and the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Mario resulted in a significant moisture increase with PWATs increasing upwards of 1.8-2.0 across most of southwest AZ. The aforementioned features also provided strong upper-level forcing to result in widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  With MLCAPE values maxing out at 1500 J/kg along with the abundant moisture, the thunderstorms were efficient rain producers, resulting in instances of flash flooding. DCAPE values ranging between 1000-1200 J/kg also resulted in instances of severe wind gusts. Further east, across Gila County, additional thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours with cell training resulting in instances of flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images and videos showed significant floodwaters in Pinal Creek, making low-water crossings impassable. The floodwater reached the base of a bridge located just south of Downtown Globe. Radar estimates showed over 2 inches of rain fell over the basin in less than 3 hours. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level troughing off the west coast promoted a pocket of upper-level divergence over south-central AZ. With MLCAPE values ranging between 250-500 J/kg and PWAT values near 1.5, scattered thunderstorm activity with heavy rainfall rates developed across portions of the Phoenix metro area and were steered northeastward into northern Maricopa County. Although the thunderstorm activity was rather unimpactful, with the flood threat limited due to the fast steering flow, training thunderstorm activity was observed across portions of northern Maricopa County. In particular, an area near Cave Creek experienced flooding impacts during the early morning hours on the 19th with some spots observing upwards of 1.0-1.5 inches. This was enough to cause some of the washes to react, with Sonoran Wash rising above alarm stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District streamflow gauge on Sonoran Wash recorded floodwaters above the pre-determined alarm stage level of 2.0 feet, with the stage peaking at 2.95 feet.  The unbridged crossing at North Valley Parkway, approximately 1 mile upstream from the gauge location became impassable. A vehicle was reportedly stuck in the floodwaters requiring the driver to be rescued. Timing estimated based on the period when water levels exceeded and then receded below the alarm stage level as well as travel time for the water to reach North Valley Parkway. No  injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Stream Gauge along Indian Bend Wash near Shea Blvd showed water levels rising above alarm stage at 2.0 feet, with flows peaking at 2.22 feet and likely resulting in some flooding along the greenbelt and low water crossings downstream. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the northern half of AZ as well as near the International Border during the afternoon hours with more isolated activity developing across the foothills and higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. All of this activity was due to strong upper-level ascent associated with a low pressure system along the west coast and another upper-level disturbance over northern Mexico. Outflow boundaries from the aforementioned activity sparked new thunderstorm activity during the evening hours across much of the south-central AZ lower deserts, including the Phoenix area. The combination of MLCAPE values between 500-1000 J/kg and DCAPE values of around 1500 J/kg supported strong to locally severe downburst wind gusts. PWATs were also near 1.5, supporting an environment conducive to high rainfall rates with these thunderstorms and thus some flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District streamflow gauge along East Fork Cave Creek near 7th Avenue in North Phoenix recorded a water level that rose above the designated alarm stage level of 3 ft, and had a peak stage of 3.55 ft at 0035MST. Any increase in water levels to or above alarm stage leads to the low-water crossing along 7th Avenue, immediately downstream of the gauge location, becoming impassable. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail reported in Miami. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on the evening of September 18th and produced locally heavy rainfall that caused washes to flow with water. Two swift water rescues occurred along the Alamo wash due to people attempting to cross in their cars. In both cases, all of the people were rescued with no injuries. The thunderstorms produced outflow winds strong enough to loft areas of blowing dust along Interstate 10 from Picacho to Eloy and reduced visibilities to less than 0.25 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swift water rescue at E Vernice St and Birch Ave along the Alamo Wash. Occupant rescued with no injury. A nearby rain gauge recorded 0.55 inches of water in 2 hours.

Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed water raging down Chisholm Avenue in Miami. A couple of large black cylinder items were swept down the road by the floodwaters. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on the afternoon and evening of September 17th. Two storms moved through the metro, producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The winds from the storms caused a tree to fall on power lines causing a power outage Additionally, thunderstorms moved northward along Interstate 10 from Tucson to Eloy, AZ causing dust to be lofted and reduced visibilities to below 900 yards for a brief period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of water overtopping curbs west of university campus. An estimated 6 inches of water were on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two individuals attempted to cross a flooded wash and got swept away. Swift water rescues occurred with one person rescued and one fatality. An estimate of 0.75 to 1.0 inches of rainfall according to MRMS. This flash flooding closed Ocotillo Road from 9 PM on the 12th to roughly 6 AM on the 13th of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 5-7 downed power poles on Attaway Road between Arizona Farms Road and Judd Road in San Tan Valley. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing based on radar. No injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed visibility less than 1/4 mile at the Promenade at Casa Grande shopping center due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic camera from the Arizona Department of Transportation showed near zero visibility along the area of State Route 347 and Casa Blanca Road due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving through the area providing strong upper-level ascent combined with MLCAPE values between 1500-3000 J/kg and PWAT values between 1.8-2.0 (associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Lorena) resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across much of western AZ and southern CA. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over Yuma County and slowly moved westward, reaching the Lower Colorado River Valley before dissipating. With this cluster of storms, damaging winds, flash flooding and areas of dense blowing dust were observed with the City of Yuma area seeing the most impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed light pole at the Dennys located along E 16th street near I-8 in Yuma. Wind gusts were estimated at around 65 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was spotted near Sahuarita, AZ. The tornado caused no damage or injury. The time and location of the tornado was estimated by radar. The landspout tornado was left unrated due to the remote location and would be difficult to survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple photos and videos of a landspout tornado spotted near Sahuarita, AZ. The tornado caused no damage or injury. The time and location of the tornado was estimated by radar. The landspout tornado was left unrated due to the remote location and would be difficult to survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several photos and videos showing quarter size hail in San Manuel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rains caused flash flooding in a couple of areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, a video of flooded washes and a flooded road. Time and specific location were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rains caused flash flooding in a couple of areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on Tonto Creek (TNRA3) measured a 3.02 ft rise in 45 minutes.  Can infer flash flooding further upstream near the Rye Creek and Tonto Creek confluence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines along Thornton Road between Peters Road and Selma Highway in Casa Grande. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic camera from the Arizona Department of Transportation along the area of State Route 87 and Hunt Highway in Chandler showed visibility of less than 1/4 mile due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to around 150 feet at the Arizona State University Research Park Facility in Tempe due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving through the area providing strong upper-level ascent combined with MLCAPE values between 1500-3000 J/kg and PWAT values between 1.8-2.0 (associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Lorena) resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across much of western AZ and southern CA. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over Yuma County and slowly moved westward, reaching the Lower Colorado River Valley before dissipating. With this cluster of storms, damaging winds, flash flooding and areas of dense blowing dust were observed with the City of Yuma area seeing the most impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourth Avenue south of 24th street in Yuma closed due to flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of quarter-sized hail at West Pinchot Avenue and North 22nd Avenue in Phoenix. Location approximated based on radar. No injuries or damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of 1 inch hail in Benson, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were near normal with Low HeatRisk. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 2 individuals. Heat was the contributing factor for both individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 3 degrees above normal, causing Low HeatRisk. The Tucson International Airport reached a high temperature of 101 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a contributing factor in the cause of death for an individual in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rain caused stream rises and flash flooding in the Grand Canyon area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a 3 ft rise on Havasu Creek.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rain caused stream rises and flash flooding in the Grand Canyon area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Park Service and USGS provided pictures and USGS stream gage data of a flash flood event that occurred in North Canyon Wash.  The stream gage showed a peak rise of 1.1 feet at 8:55 PM on September 4th, followed by a second peak rise of 0.8 feet at 2:55 AM on September 5th.  The pictures indicated higher flows of water mixed with debris and fire ash from its source in the Dragon Bravo Burn Scar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy monsoon rainfall caused some flooding in Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer measured 2.80 inches of rain at 4 NNW Paulden.  Minor flooding was reported over a private road near Feather Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via social media, hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree snapped in half and fell on North Sunridge Drive near the intersection with Manzanita Lane in Fountain Hills, blocking traffic. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving through the area providing strong upper-level ascent combined with MLCAPE values between 1500-3000 J/kg and PWAT values between 1.8-2.0 (associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Lorena) resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across much of western AZ and southern CA. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over Yuma County and slowly moved westward, reaching the Lower Colorado River Valley before dissipating. With this cluster of storms, damaging winds, flash flooding and areas of dense blowing dust were observed with the City of Yuma area seeing the most impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteenth St and Avenue B in Yuma closed due to flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries reported.

A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of power pole down along 19th Street, in between Washington and Jefferson Streets in Phoenix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of overturned semitruck along Interstate 10. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were near normal with Low HeatRisk. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a factor in the cause of death for 2 individuals. Heat was the contributing factor for both individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in Southeastern Arizona were 2 degrees below normal, causing Low HeatRisk. The Tucson International Airport reached a high temperature of 96 degrees Fahrenheit. The Pima County Medical Examiner found that heat was a contributing factor in the cause of death for an individual in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gauge on Big Chino Wash near Paulden (BCWA3) measured a 4.1 foot rise (877cfs) in about an hour and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving through the area providing strong upper-level ascent combined with MLCAPE values between 1500-3000 J/kg and PWAT values between 1.8-2.0 (associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Lorena) resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across much of western AZ and southern CA. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over Yuma County and slowly moved westward, reaching the Lower Colorado River Valley before dissipating. With this cluster of storms, damaging winds, flash flooding and areas of dense blowing dust were observed with the City of Yuma area seeing the most impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avenue 29E at the Gila River crossing closed. Start and end times of the flooding estimated based on the downstream USGS gauge going above and then below action stage. No damages or injuries reported.

Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a landspout in an open desert area of Maricopa County, as viewed from the Tonopah area. The timing and location of the landspout was unknown. Thus the location and timing was entered based primarily on radar and a best guess on the background terrain features. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station at Safford Regional Airport gusted to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms became strong enough to produce some severe downburst winds in a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ADOT site located on SR-77 at 14 mile hill (AZT12) measured a 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms became strong enough to produce some severe downburst winds in a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Johns Airport ASOS station (KSJN) measured a 64 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved westward into the Tucson Metro area the afternoon and evening of September 6th bringing flash flooding of multiple washes having at least 6 inches of moving water.  Additionally, the thunderstorm winds caused multiple power poles to be downed causing a power outage on the west side of the Tucson Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorm heavy rainfall caused several washes to flow along South San Joaquin Road between West Milkyway Drive and Bopp Road with at  least 6 inches of water and debris along the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of power poles down near San Joaquin Rd and Neal Ave due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on the evening of September 18th and produced locally heavy rainfall that caused washes to flow with water. Two swift water rescues occurred along the Alamo wash due to people attempting to cross in their cars. In both cases, all of the people were rescued with no injuries. The thunderstorms produced outflow winds strong enough to loft areas of blowing dust along Interstate 10 from Picacho to Eloy and reduced visibilities to less than 0.25 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swift water rescue at East Calle Bettelgeux Rd and South Avenida Planeta Rd along the Alamo Wash. Occupant rescued without injury. A nearby rain gauge recorded 0.55 inches of water in 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated 2 feet of water from Webber Creek flowing over the road entering Geronimo Estates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported water flowing over Fire Control Road in at least 8 spots west of Geronimo Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flash flooding along Havasu Creek at Supai leading to trapped hikers. An upstream gauge (CCTA3) showed a rise from 4.05 ft to 5.95 ft from 12:15pm to 12:45pm MST, then another peak of 6.57 ft at 2:45pm MST.  A downstream gauge (HNSA3) showed multiple peaks in streamflow from 800 to 1,200 cfs between 1:45pm and 6:00pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Agua Fria below Ash Creek county river gauge (54897) measured a 3.75 foot rise between 5:00 pm and 5:50 pm MST with a peak streamflow of 3,354 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season low pressure system moved into southeastern California and cut off from the main steering flow.  Leftover monsoon moisture from the subtropics combined with the stalled low pressure system and brought a 4 day period of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPING report was submitted of Sycamore Creek overflowing across Fire Control Road (Nf-64) near Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon storm was strong enough to produce severe size hail in Yavapai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via social media, hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed palm tree in the Safeway parking lot located along Fountain Hills Boulevard in Fountain Hills. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries reported.

The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility in the area of State Route 347 and Riggs Road due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving through the area providing strong upper-level ascent combined with MLCAPE values between 1500-3000 J/kg and PWAT values between 1.8-2.0 (associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Lorena) resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across much of western AZ and southern CA. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours over Yuma County and slowly moved westward, reaching the Lower Colorado River Valley before dissipating. With this cluster of storms, damaging winds, flash flooding and areas of dense blowing dust were observed with the City of Yuma area seeing the most impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree fell and damaged the roof of a home located in the area of 16th street and Pendergast Avenue in Yuma. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 65 mph. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters escaped the banks of Bloody Tanks Wash and came down the streets from the north leading to significant flash flooding in downtown Miami, with lots of mud and rocky debris. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active monsoon period occurred September 12th and September 13th across Southeastern Arizona. Strong to severe thunderstorms began in the late morning on September 12th and continued through the overnight hours of September 13th. Severe thunderstorms were able to produce hail ranging from 1 inch to an estimated 2.75 inches in diameter and measured wind gusts of 60 to 63 mph. Thunderstorm winds caused a semi-truck driving on Interstate 10 to be overturned, with no injury. Additionally, a landspout tornado near Vail, Arizona was reported with no injury or damage to property. Heavy rainfall due to thunderstorm activity led to water running over washes and roadways causing the death of an individual in Benson, AZ in a swift water rescue. Additionally, the running water caused damage to roadways in Benson, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ball Rd, Riley Rd, and West Coyote Song Rd in San Simon, AZ closed due to flooding. Roadways closed for an unknown duration of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through the Tucson Metro area on the evening of September 18th and produced locally heavy rainfall that caused washes to flow with water. Two swift water rescues occurred along the Alamo wash due to people attempting to cross in their cars. In both cases, all of the people were rescued with no injuries. The thunderstorms produced outflow winds strong enough to loft areas of blowing dust along Interstate 10 from Picacho to Eloy and reduced visibilities to less than 0.25 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation's dust sensor recorded 900 yards of visibility due to blowing dust at mile marker 213 on Interstate 10 near Picacho, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across Southeastern Arizona causing strong winds, hail ranging from 0.75 to 1.0 inches in diameter, and locally heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding. A swift water rescue occurred near Tombstone involving 5 individuals with no injuries. Additionally, an estimated 8 to 12 inches of water flowed underneath the Bowie underpass roadway shown on webcam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swift water rescue for 5 individuals trapped in car on Gleeson Rd. All individuals rescued with no injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level low off the CA coast and the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Mario resulted in a significant moisture increase with PWATs increasing upwards of 1.8-2.0 across most of southwest AZ. The aforementioned features also provided strong upper-level forcing to result in widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  With MLCAPE values maxing out at 1500 J/kg along with the abundant moisture, the thunderstorms were efficient rain producers, resulting in instances of flash flooding. DCAPE values ranging between 1000-1200 J/kg also resulted in instances of severe wind gusts. Further east, across Gila County, additional thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours with cell training resulting in instances of flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple washes overflowing onto Highway 95 between Quartzite and Bouse making the low water crossings impassable. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well. ||The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives. ||Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch diameter hail at the intersection of Pinnacle Peak Road and North Pima Road. No injuries or damage were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Maricopa County Flood Control District streamflow gauges along Indian Bend Wash through Scottsdale rose above action stages and flood stages, with all low-water crossings impassable. The streamflow gauge at Indian School Rd reached 3000 cfs. Multiple water rescues were performed with broadcast videos showing multiple vehicles submerged in the floodwaters. A 38-year-old male drowned after his vehicle was completely submerged and pinned against a railing after trying to drive across the Roosevelt Street low water crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level high pressure centered over NW AZ resulted an easterly steering flow across much of the southern half of AZ. Thunderstorms initially developed over the Mogollon Rim during the early to mid afternoon hours and were steered west-southwestward into the lower elevations of south-central AZ with additional activity developing from outflow boundaries. The combination of MLCAPE values peaking at around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values near 2000 J/kg resulted in a very favorable environment for strong to severe downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface weather station AZM05 in Coolidge measured a 61 mph peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued in the western, southern, eastern, and northern sections of Southeastern Arizona. The total area from western Pima County into Graham and Greenlee counties and portions of Santa Cruz, Pinal, and Cochise counties. The overall precipitation for Arizona ranking was 14th driest for the month of September. Soil moisture readings widened in range between the 1st percentile to 30th percentile ranking for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of September, Pima county ranked the 4th driest for the period of record. Soil moisture readings increased to range between the 10th through 30th percentile for the month of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across Southeastern Arizona causing strong winds, hail ranging from 0.75 to 1.0 inches in diameter, and locally heavy rainfall that led to flash flooding. A swift water rescue occurred near Tombstone involving 5 individuals with no injuries. Additionally, an estimated 8 to 12 inches of water flowed underneath the Bowie underpass roadway shown on webcam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of nickel sized hail (0.88 inch in diameter) with an observed wind gust of 51 mph nearby report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through the northwest side of the Tucson Metro on the afternoon of September 27th. The thunderstorm winds cause 5 power poles to be downed causing a power outage for 5,890 customers. A wind gust of 65 mph was recorded nearby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed 5 poles down at West Overton Rd and La Cholla Blvd and left 5890 customers left without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level low off the CA coast and the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Mario resulted in a significant moisture increase with PWATs increasing upwards of 1.8-2.0 across most of southwest AZ. The aforementioned features also provided strong upper-level forcing to result in widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  With MLCAPE values maxing out at 1500 J/kg along with the abundant moisture, the thunderstorms were efficient rain producers, resulting in instances of flash flooding. DCAPE values ranging between 1000-1200 J/kg also resulted in instances of severe wind gusts. Further east, across Gila County, additional thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours with cell training resulting in instances of flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An SUV travelling eastbound on I-8 veered off the road, possibly due to hydroplaning, and landed upside down in a flowing wash. The two occupants were able to escape the vehicle unharmed. Timing estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from San Carlos Apache Police Department showed a water rescue of 5 kids and their dog who were trapped out on an island in the middle of Gilson Wash near Waco Rd. The police department received the calls for help around 2040MST. The water moving through the wash was up to the waists of the kids at the time of the rescue. A gauge several miles upstream on Gilson Wash began rising just after 1900MST and reached a peak stage of 4.2 ft at 2036MST. The floodwaters originated from a storm that produced around 2 inches of rain near Cutter. No injuries were reported.

A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days.||Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. Damage cost estimates were in the millions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Catastrophic and deadly flash flooding occurred in the Globe area following strong to severe thunderstorms that produced very high rainfall rates. Observed gauge reports and doppler radar rainfall estimates measured approximately 2 inches of rain within 30 minutes in the Globe area. A stream gauge along the typically dry Pinal Creek, just south of Downtown Globe, recorded a dramatic 6-foot rise in water in 30 minutes between 1825MST and 1855MST.  This flood wave quickly moved through downtown Globe, overtopping bridges, inundating businesses, and sweeping away dozens of vehicles. Many sloped streets also became raging currents. Additionally, around 800 propane tanks were swept into the floodwaters from a distribution center along the creek. Several dozen homes and businesses were inundated with water, with some experiencing water levels of two to three feet deep. Floodwaters traveled over 15 miles north of Globe, within Pinal Creek, all the way to the Salt River and Roosevelt Lake with additional flooding impacts experienced in the Wheatfields area. A USGS gauge on Pinal Creek just south of the Salt River recorded an 8 ft water level rise and came within half a foot of reaching the gauge's historical peak. In the aftermath of the flooding, approximately 288 tons of trash and debris as well as 800 cubic yards of dirt were cleared. There were also over 60 vehicles damaged or destroyed, with some of them wedged under bridges. The flooding resulted in three fatalities, all from vehicles swept away. In response to the catastrophic flash flooding, Globe's Mayor, Al Gameros, declared a State of Emergency for Globe on the afternoon of the 27th followed shortly by an Arizona State of Emergency declaration from Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs. Preliminary damage assessments performed in the subsequent days had estimated damage costs of approximately $25 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides.
